8fabc4f29422686ab0cbac43714a954756c9fe30.tech: Notion scaled AI Q&A vector search from November 2023 through 2026, moving from a dual-path indexing setup (Spark batch chunking/embeddings and Kafka real-time page edits) on dedicated vector database pods to a multi-vendor serverless architecture. They migrated vector storage to turbopuffer, added DynamoDB-based page state caching with xxHash change detection, and shifted embeddings from external APIs to self-hosted Ray/Anyscale models with Ray Serve for query-time embedding.
8fabc4f29422686ab0cbac43714a954756c9fe30.nontech: Notion built and scaled the vector search behind its AI Q&A across millions of workspaces, starting at launch in November 2023 and continuing through 2026. They handled rapid growth while cutting costs by 90%, improving query latency from 70–100ms to 50–70ms, clearing a multi-million workspace waitlist, and reducing data volume by 70% through smarter updates.

ecafce8d72db850ca81b99f373061e7562989327.tech: Solo.io built and open-sourced agentgateway to mediate traffic between internal AI agents, MCP (Model Context Protocol) servers, and LLM providers. The gateway centralizes MCP multiplexing via configuration (multiple stateless MCP backends exposed through a single /mcp endpoint), JWT authentication with JWKS validation, and gateway-level timeout policies. It also adds observability by enriching metrics/logs at the gateway, tracking LLM usage and costs per user and organization, and enforcing JWT-based authentication plus authorization rules for LLM access. Agentgateway supports model routing and request transformation for multi-tenant Vertex AI projects using organization headers and path-based model extraction.
ecafce8d72db850ca81b99f373061e7562989327.nontech: Solo.io used a centralized gateway to manage how their growing set of AI agents connects to MCP servers and LLM providers. Instead of changing each agent or backend, the gateway adds security checks, consistent access rules, and visibility into who is using what. It also tracks LLM usage and costs by user and organization, helping them control spending and scale their agent-based support and coding workflows.

da8100c354dbf4fb9000740f730699e64190e525.tech: Daisy Health built a production event-driven RAG system using Anthropic’s Model Context Protocol (MCP) on AWS. S3 uploads trigger AWS Lambda to call an MCP vectorization tool, which parses PDFs, generates embeddings with AWS Titan, and stores vectors in LanceDB. A chatbot queries MCP search tools with elicitation for plan status and employer context, secured via JWT tokens from AWS Cognito through an API gateway.
da8100c354dbf4fb9000740f730699e64190e525.nontech: Daisy Health, a healthcare benefits marketplace, had trouble keeping chatbot answers up to date because benefits information arrived as PDFs and required manual reindexing. They added an automated system that ingests provider documents, updates the searchable data in real time, and lets employees ask questions and get personalized recommendations grounded in their plan documents, with authentication handled through AWS Cognito and JWT tokens.

2018f38ea2643073e651c7332210ae30e82bc557.tech: Twin Sun’s Scarif “dark factory” uses Claude Code agents orchestrated by a Rails backend on AWS. Jira tickets feed a multi-stage pipeline: planning, implementation, specialized PR review, evaluation with up to five iteration cycles, verification (CI checks, automated tests, security checks, accessibility scans), and configurable deployment with optional manual gates. Worker nodes run agents concurrently via a harness; a custom Claude Code plugin installs on workers and adds technology-specific rule sets to Claude.md based on repo detection. MCP integration uses Atlassian MCP for Jira bulk task creation/specification. Visual design review extracts design tokens from Figma/Zeplin, checks Tailwind configuration, runs the app, and uses Playwright to extract computed CSS; it also decomposes screens into regions/components for comparison. A pre-warming step scans for open ports above 3000 to avoid port conflicts.
2018f38ea2643073e651c7332210ae30e82bc557.nontech: Twin Sun, a Nashville-based software development agency, built Scarif, an autonomous “software factory” to scale delivery across multiple client projects while keeping code quality consistent. They introduced AI step-by-step inside their existing workflow, starting with PR review and expanding to code generation, testing, and deployment. They reported about 70% of pull requests approved without human intervention while maintaining their standards. They also added a more careful, token-based approach for design review and kept human approval gates where needed.

86244708f5796c9834e480a2223a1ab76d45697e.tech: Liquid AI builds small edge-focused LFM models (350M–24B) with architecture and training optimized for limited memory and latency. LFM2 uses gated short convolution blocks plus grouped query attention, profiled on target CPUs and mobile devices. Pre-training uses 28T tokens, followed by supervised fine-tuning, preference alignment (on-policy length-normalized DPO), and reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards and n-gram repetition penalties.
86244708f5796c9834e480a2223a1ab76d45697e.nontech: Liquid AI focuses on making language models work on edge devices with limited memory and compute, such as smartphones and in-car systems. Its LFM models are designed for specific tasks like tool use and data extraction, not general chat. The company also targets reliability issues like “doom looping,” aiming for faster throughput, lower memory use, and dependable on-device performance.

a9f0dc273fb535967ea73bc74a3f962624e8192c.tech: HumanLayer evolved production AI coding agents from RPI (Research-Plan-Implement) to CRISPY (Context-Research-Implement-Structure-Plan-and-Yeah). They decomposed monolithic prompts into multiple discrete stages with fewer than 40 instructions per prompt, using multiple context windows per stage. CRISPY expands to seven stages: Questions, Research, Design, Structure/Outline, Plan, Work/Implement, and Pull Request, with static markdown artifacts for alignment and mandatory code review.
a9f0dc273fb535967ea73bc74a3f962624e8192c.nontech: HumanLayer built a workflow for AI coding agents meant to work in real production teams. Their earlier approach (RPI) worked for expert engineers but didn’t scale because behavior became inconsistent and teams lacked enough oversight, leading to code quality problems. CRISPY uses smaller steps, shared review documents, and requires code review to keep quality high while improving productivity.

5d4fbc3da3980c88830492a34eea205d8b468bfd.tech: Cursor replaced a git worktrees feature (~15,000 lines) with markdown-based agent skills (~40 lines) using agent skills and sub-agents. The worktree skill instructs models to create worktrees, run setup scripts, and stay within the checkout; best-of-n spawns sub-agents per model, waits, then grades in a table. Commands enable server-side prompt control. Challenges include model reliability staying in designated worktrees, especially for smaller models and long sessions. Cursor is addressing this with Braintrust evaluation harnesses (two scorers) and reinforcement learning/training for Composer, plus continued prompt refinement and system reminders.
5d4fbc3da3980c88830492a34eea205d8b468bfd.nontech: Cursor rebuilt its git worktrees feature using prompt-based “skills” instead of a large code implementation. The change reduced maintenance and enabled new user capabilities like switching into a worktree mid-chat and supporting multi-repo setups with separate pull requests. The tradeoff is less predictable behavior: models can sometimes work in the wrong checkout, especially over long sessions. Cursor is improving reliability using evaluation and training, and refining prompts over time.

0b4f907ef513ef13af7e18673fca4ae3add665d0.tech: Amazon Prime Video addressed context bloat from centralized MCP servers exposing hundreds of tools to agents. They implemented progressive tool discovery using MCP notifications and session tracking via an MCP session ID header. Only a single “find tools” tool is exposed at initialization; agents call it by problem category, then fetch updated tool lists after tools list changed notifications, enabling mid-session tool swapping.
0b4f907ef513ef13af7e18673fca4ae3add665d0.nontech: Amazon Prime Video found that giving AI agents access to a centralized set of hundreds of tools made them slower and less reliable, with more hallucinations. Their team changed the approach so agents start with only one “find tools” option, then load just a few tools that match the task category. This improved performance while keeping centralized tool management across teams.

941260c95dd7eeeb6eda6c374f7f82bce71ae8e5.tech: The discussion contrasts context engineering—manually crafting prompts and tool calls in a recursive loop—with harness engineering, where models are reinforcement-learned to work optimally with specific tool sets and execution environments. It covers nested while loops, outer harness orchestration (e.g., Ralph/Gas Town), MCP integration, evaluation-driven development, and human-in-the-loop review to prevent eval overfitting.
941260c95dd7eeeb6eda6c374f7f82bce71ae8e5.nontech: The case study explains how production LLM systems evolved from hand-built prompt-and-tool workflows to “harness” systems that are trained to work well with the tools they’ll use. It emphasizes choosing between custom infrastructure and existing frameworks, using evaluations to guide improvements, and keeping humans involved to catch issues that metrics can miss.

a88405bc906a91afa62691e3afdb05c31dab2eb0.tech: Trigger.dev addresses durable LLM agent execution by splitting durability into an append-only context log and VM-level snapshot/restore for the execution layer. They first used CRIU (checkpoint/restore in userspace) for process-level snapshots, then switched to Firecracker micro VMs to snapshot entire machines. Seekable compression reduces snapshots from ~512MB to ~14MB, enabling sub-second snapshots and ~200ms restores, with ~15,000 VM starts/min. They bundle the approach in FC Run, a Docker-like CLI running containers inside Firecracker VMs with snapshotting/restore.
a88405bc906a91afa62691e3afdb05c31dab2eb0.nontech: Trigger.dev’s case study focuses on how to run LLM-powered agents reliably in production when sessions can last hours or days. It explains why traditional stateless setups and replay-style workflows don’t fit. Their solution keeps conversation history in an append-only log and preserves the agent’s working environment by snapshotting and restoring lightweight VMs, reducing idle costs while maintaining state.

896c40568457677bdd40c17d489820e4db32ca53.tech: Shopify’s River is an AI coding agent integrated into the company’s monorepo workflow. It operates exclusively in public Slack channels (declines direct messages), can read code, run tests, write code, open pull requests, query the data warehouse, and examine production traces. River uses skills, per-channel zones/instructions, and a memory system that continuously learns and un-learns critical information.
896c40568457677bdd40c17d489820e4db32ca53.nontech: Shopify built River, an AI coding agent that works only in public Slack channels. The goal was a “teaching workshop” environment where employees learn by watching others use the agent, helping ideas and problem-solving approaches spread across the organization. In 30 days, 5,938 employees used River across 4,450 channels, and its PR merge rate rose from 36% to 77% over two months.
